title: Reversible Flawed Merge Sort on Doubly Linked List

statement.markdown:
Given a doubly linked list, you want to perform a Reversible Merge Sort on it. The merge sort works as follows:
It takes two halves of the list and sorts them recursively.
Then it merges the two sorted halves, ensuring that the result is still sorted.
However, your code has a bug that prevents it from merging the two sorted halves correctly.
statement.inputFormat: You will be given a doubly linked list with n nodes, where each node contains an integer value. The nodes are 1-indexed.
statement.outputFormat: You should return the head of the sorted doubly linked list.
statement.notes: The merge function should be reversible, meaning that after sorting, given a node, you should be able to find the original position of that node in the unsorted list.

examples.explanation: The input is a doubly linked list with four nodes (1, 2, 3, 4)
The input is a doubly linked list with five nodes (5, 2, 8, 1, 3)

hints.text: The problem with your merge function is that it doesn't correctly merge two sorted halves.

editorial.title: Reversible Merge Sort
editorial.intuition: To perform a reversible merge sort, you need to split the list into two halves, sort them, and then merge them back together.
editorial.bruteForce: You can use a simple merge sort algorithm to sort the list, but this will not be reversible.
editorial.betterSolution: To make the merge function reversible, you need to keep track of the original position of each node in the unsorted list.
editorial.optimalSolution: Create a function that splits the list into two halves, sorts them recursively, and then merges them back together while keeping track of the original position of each node.
editorial.proofOfCorrectness: We can prove that the merge function is reversible by showing that it correctly maps each node to its original position in the unsorted list.
editorial.dryRun: To dry run this solution, you can create a small input and manually go through the sorting process to verify that it is reversible.
commonMistakes: not keeping track of original position
